turns.text: Hello Alejandrina, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat, tucking a strand of hair behind my ear*

Yeah, of course. I appreciate you saying that about there being no right or wrong answers—I'm not sure I always believe that about myself these days, you know? 

*gives a small, self-aware laugh*

This is... it's strange being on this side of things. I spend my days talking to other pregnant people about what to expect, explaining the science, trying to calm their nerves. And here I am, supposedly knowing exactly what's happening in my body right now, and I'm a complete mess about it.

I'm about twelve weeks, I think? Maybe thirteen. This is my second pregnancy, which people seem to think means I should be more relaxed this time. Instead, I'm more anxious. The first time around, I didn't know enough to be scared. Now I *know* everything that could go wrong—not in a paranoid way, but like... genuinely, medically know it—and that knowledge sits on my chest some mornings.

*pauses, looking down at hands*

What's been hardest is the shift in how I see myself. I'm used to being the expert in the room, the one with answers. Now I'm just... a woman carrying something I can't fully control, and I'm trying to make peace with that. My midwife is great, but I catch myself speaking her language back to her instead of just being honest about being terrified.

What else would you like to know?
Tell me about the people who were part of your world when you first started thinking about having a baby — who came to mind, and what role did each of them play in those early conversations?

*takes a breath, eyes getting a bit distant*

Oh. That's... that's actually a harder question than it sounds.

*pauses for a long moment*

Honestly? When I first started thinking seriously about this, it was mostly me. Alone at night with my laptop, looking at fertility clinic websites, running the numbers in my head. I'm single, and I made the choice to do this on my own—which sounds very deliberate and empowered when I say it like that, but the truth is messier. There was someone I thought might be... and then there wasn't. And I had to decide: wait for the right person, or trust myself to do this anyway?

My mom was the first person I told, and that conversation was... *trails off* ...complicated. She's supportive now, but there was definitely some initial worry about whether I was being realistic. She keeps asking if I'm sleeping enough, if I'm eating enough, if my job situation is stable. Which it isn't, by the way—that's its own nightmare.

My best friend has been incredible. She's the one who came with me to that first ultrasound before I'd told almost anyone. Seeing her tear up when we heard the heartbeat... that made it real in a different way.

*looks up*

But mostly? I've had to build my own village from scratch. That's been lonely sometimes. There's no partner in the next room. It's just me deciding what happens next.
Who else was involved in those early conversations that perhaps surprised you — someone you didn't expect to turn to?

*leans back, thinking*

You know, my ob-gyn from my first pregnancy. I wasn't planning to reach out to her initially—I figured I'd just schedule an appointment like anyone else. But I ran into her at a coffee shop, of all places, and we got to talking. I almost didn't mention it, but something about running into her felt like... I don't know, permission? To acknowledge this wasn't just some abstract plan anymore.

*fidgets with hands*

She was surprisingly candid in a way that actually helped. Not in a clinical way—in a *real* way. She told me about her own journey, asked pointed questions about my support system, my job situation. She didn't sugarcoat anything, but she also didn't make me feel like I was crazy for doing this solo. That mattered more than I expected it to.

And then there's my supervisor at work. I didn't want to tell her, obviously—I was still figuring things out myself—but I had to because of the morning sickness and the fatigue. I was terrified she'd see me as less committed, less serious. Instead, she asked about my healthcare access and whether I needed any schedule adjustments. She actually *asked*. Like I was a person with needs, not just an employee.

*shrugs slightly*

I think I was surprised by how many people actually... cared? Once I stopped assuming judgment and just let people know what was happening. Though that vulnerability still doesn't come easy to me.

Tell me about your partner's involvement — or the involvement of someone close to you — when you were deciding whether and when to try for a baby. How did those conversations go, and who held the most influence?

*expression shifts, becomes more guarded*

Yeah, so... there isn't a partner. That's the thing I'm still processing, actually.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

There was someone. About two years ago, we were talking about what the future might look like together. He seemed open to it, or at least I thought he did. But when it got real—when I actually started saying, "Okay, I'm thinking about this seriously"—he realized he wasn't ready. Maybe he never was. And I had to make a choice: wait for him to figure it out, or move forward with what I wanted.

*looks away briefly*

I chose myself. Which sounds empowering, and maybe it is, but it's also... lonely. There's no one to talk to at 3 AM when I'm spiraling about whether I'm making the right decision. No one to feel this with me in the way that a partnership would offer.

*voice gets quieter*

The person who held the most influence was actually my therapist, if I'm being honest. She helped me untangle what I actually wanted versus what I thought I *should* want. Whether I needed a partner to be a good mother, or whether I could build a life around this baby on my own terms.

*meets your eyes*

I'm not going to pretend it's not hard. But I'm doing it. And now I'm pregnant, and it's real, and I'm still figuring out what that means for how I move through the world.
Were there moments when you and your partner — or people close to you — wanted different things? How did you navigate that?

*shifts uncomfortably, jaw tightening slightly*

I mean... yes. That's kind of the whole story, isn't it?

*rubs face*

With him, it wasn't some dramatic conflict where we sat down and had it out. It was more insidious than that. We'd talk about "someday," and I'd think he meant it the way I meant it. But when I started actually planning—looking at timelines, thinking about finances, my career trajectory—he got quieter. Then he admitted he was scared. Scared of the responsibility, scared of losing his freedom, scared of... a lot of things.

And I had to decide if I was willing to wait for him to not be scared. If I was willing to put my biological clock and my own desires on hold indefinitely. The answer was no.

*voice gets steadier*

With my mom, it's different. She *wants* this grandchild, but she also wants me to have it "the right way"—with stability, with a partner, with all my ducks in a row. We've had some sharp conversations about whether I'm being reckless. I don't think she sees it as me choosing myself; she sees it as me settling for less.

*meets your gaze directly*

But here's the thing—I navigated it by accepting that I couldn't make everyone comfortable. I could listen to their concerns, take what was useful, and then do what felt true for me anyway. That's been the hardest part, actually. Not the logistics of being a single mother. The guilt of disappointing people I love.

Think about the healthcare professionals you saw before you became pregnant — a GP, a fertility specialist, or anyone else. Tell me about those relationships. Who felt most helpful, and who felt more distant?

*nods slowly, considering*

I saw my GP first, which was... awkward. I basically said, "I'm thinking about pursuing pregnancy as a single woman, and I need to know if there are any health concerns I should address." And he got this look on his face like I'd just told him I was planning to climb Everest without a guide.

*laughs dryly*

He wasn't *unkind*, but he kept pivoting the conversation to "have you considered waiting?" and "what about your support system?" Like those were medical questions he needed to answer, not personal ones I'd already agonized over a thousand times. He did refer me to a fertility clinic, but the whole interaction felt like I was being gently discouraged rather than supported.

The fertility specialist was different—much more businesslike, which I actually appreciated. No judgment, just: here's what we can do, here's the timeline, here's the cost. Clinical, yes, but also... respectful? She treated it like a legitimate medical choice.

*expression softens*

My midwife now—she's the one who's been most helpful. She asked about my fears without trying to talk me out of them. She didn't assume I was making a mistake. And crucially, she lets me be *both* the person who understands the medicine and the person who's terrified. She doesn't make me choose between those versions of myself.

*pauses*

The distance I felt from my GP? That stung. Like he couldn't separate his own beliefs about how things "should" be from his job of just... helping me stay healthy.

Tell me about the people — online or offline — who you turned to for information and reassurance before you got pregnant. Who was most influential, and how did their advice or stories shape what you believed about pregnancy?

*leans forward slightly, becoming more animated*

Oh man, okay. So professionally, I'm constantly reading research, following evidence-based sources—ACOG guidelines, peer-reviewed studies on maternal health outcomes. That's my world. But when I started actually *preparing* for this, I realized all that clinical knowledge was both helpful and... suffocating.

*gestures expressively*

I found these online communities for single mothers by choice. Reddit threads, Facebook groups, Instagram accounts. And I was *hungry* for those stories. Not the sanitized medical information, but the real stuff—women talking about how lonely it was at the anatomy scan, how they handled telling their families, what actually worked for childcare. That was more valuable to me than any guideline could be.

*expression becomes more thoughtful*

There's this one blogger I followed obsessively—a woman in her mid-thirties who'd done IVF solo. Her honesty about the grief mixed in with the joy? That gave me permission to feel complicated things. She made it seem less like I was broken or settling, and more like I was choosing a different path.

*pauses, self-aware*

The tricky part is that I'd then second-guess those stories against the clinical data in my head. Like, someone would share their experience and I'd think, "Well, that's anecdotal, the statistics actually say..." *laughs* I became my own worst enemy sometimes.

What I *wish* I'd found was more nuance. Stories from women who were genuinely ambivalent, not just triumphant or tragic.
Who else was involved in pointing you toward those sources — a friend, a family member, or did you find them yourself?

*settles back, thinking*

I found most of them myself, honestly. Late nights scrolling, which probably wasn't great for my mental health, but it felt necessary at the time. Like I was doing research for the biggest decision of my life.

*pauses*

My best friend was the one who actually *intervened* at one point. She noticed I was going down this rabbit hole of forums and blogs, and she basically said, "Okay, you need to stop reading other people's stories and start figuring out what *your* story is." Which sounds simple, but it was weirdly profound. I was so busy trying to find a roadmap from other single mothers that I wasn't trusting my own judgment.

*fidgets slightly*

She actually sent me a few things—links to blogs, a podcast about motherhood that wasn't specifically about being single. But more importantly, she made me talk to her about what I was reading instead of just absorbing it in isolation. She'd ask, "Does that resonate with you?" or "Does that scare you?" instead of just letting me doom-scroll.

*looks up*

My mom, interestingly, hasn't really pointed me toward resources. She's more the type to worry about what I'm reading and whether it's making me anxious. Which it was, honestly. But I needed to do that work myself.

I think what I was really searching for online wasn't information—it was permission. And evidence that I wasn't crazy for doing this.

Tell me about the women in your family — your mother, grandmothers, sisters, or aunts — and how their experiences of pregnancy and motherhood influenced your own thinking before you even started trying.

*takes a breath, expression becoming more reflective*

My mom had me at twenty-eight, married to my dad. It was... the conventional path, you know? She was a teacher, stayed home for a few years, then went back. She loves being a mother, but I think she also sacrificed a lot of ambition to make that work. She's never said it outright, but I can feel it sometimes—this sense that she chose motherhood *or* career, not both, not on her own terms.

*pauses, picking words carefully*

My grandmother—my mom's mom—was much more independent. She had my mom young, raised her mostly alone after my grandfather left. She was a nurse, worked nights, did what she had to do. My mom talks about her with this mix of admiration and... concern? Like she's proud but also worried my mom had to grow up too fast.

*voice gets quieter*

I don't have sisters, which I think matters. I didn't grow up watching other women navigate their own reproductive choices. It was just my mom's story, which was loving but also constrained in ways I didn't fully understand until I was older.

*looks directly at you*

What I internalized was that motherhood required you to make certain sacrifices or compromises. Either you had a partner to share the load, or you were doing it alone and struggling. I didn't see a third option—someone choosing it deliberately, on purpose, without apology.

My grandmother gave me permission to try. My mom made me terrified of doing it alone.
How did their stories — whether positive or difficult — show up in your own expectations or fears?

*eyes get a bit glassy, takes a moment*

Oh god, this is where it gets real, isn't it?

*voice becomes quieter*

My mom's sacrifice narrative? That's in my head constantly. She was so capable, so smart, and I watched her scale back her ambitions to make room for motherhood and marriage. She doesn't regret me—I know that—but there's this undercurrent of "what if?" And now I'm terrified I'm going to do the same thing, except alone. Like, at least she had my dad's income to fall back on. I'm out here trying to figure out how to keep my career moving while growing a human and building a life solo.

*rubs face*

My grandmother's resilience was inspiring, but also... it normalized struggle in a way that scares me. She made it work, yes, but at what cost? She was exhausted. I remember her being tired in a bone-deep way. And I think, "Is that just what single motherhood is? Is that my future?"

*pauses, voice steadier but more vulnerable*

What I didn't inherit from either of them was the belief that I could have it all without compromise. Or that asking for help wasn't weakness. They both just... muscled through. And I'm trying not to do that, but it's hardwired in me—this idea that if I'm doing this alone, I need to *prove* I can handle it all perfectly.

*meets your eyes*

That's probably my biggest fear, honestly. Not that I'll fail, but that I'll succeed at the cost of everything else that matters to me.
Tell me about a time — if there was one — when trying to conceive felt harder than you expected. Who was around you during that period, and how did those relationships change or hold steady?

*shifts uncomfortably, takes a long pause*

I haven't actually tried to conceive yet. I'm still in that liminal space—I've done the planning, the medical clearance, the soul-searching. But I haven't actually started the process of insemination or IVF or whatever it's going to be.

*laughs, but there's tension in it*

Which is its own kind of hard, honestly. It's this thing I've decided I want, I've told people about, and now I'm... waiting. Waiting for my job situation to stabilize, waiting for the right timing, waiting for my own anxiety to calm down enough to actually move forward.

*voice becomes more reflective*

During this waiting period, my best friend has been the steady one. She checks in, asks if I'm ready yet, doesn't push but also doesn't let me disappear into my own head about it. My mom, though—she's oscillated. Some weeks she's encouraging, other weeks she seems almost relieved that I haven't done it yet, like maybe I'll change my mind.

*expression tightens slightly*

What's been hardest is the isolation of it. I can't really talk about this at work without making things complicated. And I'm not in a relationship where I can process it with a partner. So it's just... me and my therapist and my best friend, mostly.

*looks up*

And now, somehow, I'm already pregnant. Which means all that waiting, all that deliberation—it's already behind me. And I'm discovering I wasn't actually ready in the way I thought I'd be.
Who else was involved in supporting you — or perhaps adding to the pressure — at that time?

*pauses, looking conflicted*

Honestly, at this point, I'm not sure there's a clear "support" versus "pressure" divide. It's all kind of tangled up.

*rubs temples*

My therapist was probably the most neutral presence, which sounds clinical, but what I mean is she wasn't invested in the outcome. She just kept asking me to examine my motivations, my fears, my readiness. No judgment either way.

My supervisor at work—she added pressure inadvertently. Not maliciously, but she kept asking about my long-term plans, whether I was thinking about my career trajectory. And I couldn't tell her the truth, so I just kept saying, "Oh, I'm figuring things out." That felt like lying while also trying to make a life-altering decision.

*voice gets tighter*

And then there's this unspoken pressure from the fertility clinic itself. Once you're in their system, there's this momentum, you know? They're scheduling consultations, running tests, sending you paperwork. It all feels inevitable, like the machinery is already in motion whether you're ready or not.

*looks down*

My mom was... complicated during this time. I think she was hoping I'd get cold feet, back out, meet someone. There was this underlying current of "are you *sure* about this?" that felt less like genuine concern and more like she was waiting for me to come to my senses.

*meets your eyes*

Nobody was really just... sitting with me in the uncertainty. Everyone had a stake in how this turned out.
Tell me about the midwives, doctors, and other healthcare professionals who were part of your pregnancy care. Which relationships felt most important to you, and why?

*leans forward, energy shifting*

My midwife now is... she's become really important to me. Her name is Sarah, and I think what makes her different is that she doesn't treat me like I'm a cautionary tale or a special case because I'm single. She also doesn't treat me like I'm just another patient checking boxes.

*gestures expressively*

In that first appointment, I caught myself explaining my pregnancy in clinical terms—"I'm thirty, first pregnancy was uncomplicated, medium risk flagged on my chart"—and she gently interrupted me. She said, "That's helpful context, but tell me how *you're* feeling." And I just... broke down. Because nobody had asked me that. Everyone wanted the data or the plan, not the actual terror underneath.

*voice softens*

She's also very clear about boundaries in a way I appreciate. She won't let me hide behind medical knowledge. When I tried to self-diagnose some nausea using pharmacology, she basically said, "I need you to be a pregnant person right now, not a health educator." That was weirdly liberating.

*expression becomes more guarded*

The OB I saw before—the one I ran into at the coffee shop—she was helpful initially, but I realized she kind of had one foot in my professional world and one in my patient world, and that muddied things. It was harder to be vulnerable with someone who knew me as a colleague.

*pauses*

My GP still feels distant. He never quite made peace with my choice, and I can feel that in the room with us.
Who else was involved in your care that you'd like to mention — community midwives, consultants, support workers, interpreters?

*thinks for a moment*

Honestly, right now it's pretty minimal. Just Sarah, my midwife, and I have one follow-up scheduled with the OB for the anatomy scan since I'm flagged as medium risk. But I haven't actually met with a consultant yet.

*pauses, considering*

I've been thinking about whether I need a doula or a birth worker. Someone who isn't clinical but who's trained, you know? Someone who can be present in a way that's separate from my best friend's role. Because as much as I love her, I also don't want to burden her with being my only support person during labor.

*shifts uncomfortably*

The thing is, affording that feels like a luxury I'm not sure I can manage right now, especially with the job situation being unstable. I've looked into community resources, sliding scale options, but I haven't actually committed to anything yet. Which probably means I will, eventually, because I'm the type of person who researches obsessively and then acts on it.

*voice becomes more reflective*

What I've realized is that I'm still building my village, and a lot of it feels makeshift. Like, I'm piecing together a care team from whoever I can access and whoever's willing to show up. It's not the coordinated, integrated experience I imagine other pregnant people have with partners and established family networks.

*looks at you directly*

I think I'm going to need more support as this progresses. I'm just not sure what that looks like yet.
Tell me about your partner's — or your closest support person's — involvement during your pregnancy appointments and day-to-day experience. How did their presence or absence shape things for you?

*takes a sharp breath, then settles back*

My best friend came to the ultrasound where we heard the heartbeat. That was the only appointment so far where I had someone with me, and it completely changed the experience.

*voice gets softer, more emotional*

When that galloping sound came through the speaker, I looked over at her and she was already crying. And suddenly it wasn't just this terrifying, abstract thing happening inside my body—it was real and witnessed. She grabbed my hand, and I felt like I could actually breathe for the first time in weeks.

*pauses, expression becoming more complex*

But here's the hard part: she can't be at every appointment. She has her own life, her own job. And I don't want to be the kind of friend who expects her to rearrange everything for me. So most of my appointments, I'm going alone. Sitting in those waiting rooms by myself, hearing clinical observations without anyone to process them with afterward.

*voice becomes quieter, more vulnerable*

The absence shapes everything, if I'm honest. I'm taking notes on my phone because there's nobody else to remember what Sarah said. I'm Googling things alone at night instead of having someone tell me to stop catastrophizing. And the day-to-day—the exhaustion, the nausea, the weird body changes—I'm experiencing all of that solo.

*looks directly at you*

It's not that I regret my choice. But the loneliness of it... that hits different than I expected. Especially now that it's real.

Tell me about the people — friends, family, online communities — who you turned to most during your pregnancy when you had questions or worries. How did those relationships work alongside your formal care?

*settles into the question, becoming more animated*

My best friend is my first call for everything. She knows me well enough to distinguish between "I'm spiraling with anxiety" and "I actually have a legitimate concern." Like, last week I was convinced something was wrong because my nausea disappeared for two days, and she talked me down without making me feel stupid. But she also knows when to say, "Okay, you need to call Sarah about this."

*gestures expressively*

Sarah, my midwife, gets the medical questions that go beyond what I can logic my way through. Though honestly, half the time I'm sitting in her office already knowing the answer from my own research, and I have to stop myself from just... stating facts at her. It's weird being on this side of that dynamic.

*pauses, becoming more thoughtful*

The online communities have been helpful but also dangerous for me. I'll read someone's story about bleeding in the first trimester and suddenly I'm convinced that's happening to me, even though I have zero symptoms. My brain starts connecting dots that don't exist. So I've had to be more intentional about when I engage with those spaces.

*voice gets quieter*

What's missing is someone in my immediate physical life who just... gets it. My mom asks about appointments, but every conversation feels like she's assessing whether I'm handling this "responsibly." It's not a safe space to say, "I'm terrified and I don't know if I'm going to be a good mother."

*looks at you*

My therapist gets some of that, but she costs money and I can only see her every other week. My friend bears a lot of the emotional labor, probably more than is fair.

Tell me about a time during your pregnancy when you had to make a significant decision about a test, a procedure, or a care option. Who was involved in that decision with you, and how did each person's input land?

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting a bit distant*

The "medium risk" flag. That was the significant moment for me.

*sits forward*

I got the results back from my initial screening—some combination of age, hormone levels, maybe the ultrasound measurements—and suddenly there's this label attached to my pregnancy. And my immediate instinct was to pull up every study about what "medium risk" actually means, what the false positive rates are, what the true risk of chromosomal abnormalities is at my age. I was trying to logic my way out of the anxiety.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

Sarah asked me what I wanted to do about further testing—amniocentesis, more detailed screening, whatever. And I froze. Because the clinical answer and the emotional answer were completely different. Clinically, I know the risks of the procedure versus the actual risk of abnormalities. Emotionally, I was terrified either way.

*rubs face*

I called my best friend, and she asked me the right question: "What would help you feel safer?" Not "what does the data say?" Just... what would actually make me feel okay moving forward? And I realized I needed more information, but I also needed to sit with the uncertainty without drowning in it.

*pauses*

My mom, when I told her, immediately went into worry mode. Like, "Oh my god, what does that mean? Are you going to do the amnio? You have to be careful." Which made me feel worse, not better. Like the risk was suddenly more real because she was scared.

*looks at you directly*

I'm still deciding. Sarah said we don't have to rush. But that decision is... it's mine to make alone, and that's terrifying.

Tell me about how your relationships with people outside the healthcare system — your employer, your community, your cultural or faith community — shaped your pregnancy experience. Who was supportive, who added pressure?

*expression becomes more complicated, conflicted*

My employer is... complicated. My supervisor has been surprisingly decent about accommodations—flexible scheduling when I'm exhausted, understanding when I need to leave for appointments. But there's this unspoken tension underneath it all. I haven't told her I'm pregnant yet because I'm still figuring out my job situation. The position I'm in is technically temporary, and I don't know if I'll have stable employment when I need maternity leave.

*voice gets tighter*

That adds this layer of pressure where I'm trying to prove my value at work while also managing a pregnancy that's increasingly hard to hide. I'm exhausted in meetings, I'm running to the bathroom constantly, and I'm terrified someone's going to put it together before I'm ready to tell them. That uncertainty is almost worse than the judgment would be.

*pauses, looking uncomfortable*

As for community or faith—I don't really have that. I'm not particularly religious, and I don't have a tight-knit cultural community here in Boston. I moved for work, and I built my life around professional networks, not community roots. Which means I'm missing that traditional safety net that a lot of people have. No church ladies bringing casseroles, no extended family invested in the outcome.

*voice becomes quieter*

That's isolating. Like, my coworkers don't know, my extended family barely knows what's happening in my life, and I'm just... navigating this mostly alone in a city where I'm not deeply embedded anywhere.

*meets your eyes*

It's made me realize how much I took for granted before—the assumption that community would just be there.
How did that affect your sense of identity — were there moments when being pregnant felt in tension with other parts of who you are?

*leans back, expression becoming introspective*

All the time. Constantly, actually.

*laughs, but there's tension in it*

I built my entire identity around being competent, knowledgeable, in control. I'm the person who educates other people about their bodies. I'm analytical, I solve problems, I don't need help. And then pregnancy hits and suddenly I'm... leaking, exhausted, unable to fit into my clothes, crying at commercials. It's like watching someone else inhabit my body.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

There's this moment every morning where I have to choose between being Alejandrina the health educator and Alejandrina the pregnant woman. And they feel mutually exclusive right now. When I'm in professional spaces, I'm hyperaware of trying to perform normalcy—like I need to prove I'm still sharp, still competent, that this pregnancy isn't going to define me or diminish my credibility.

*pauses, picking at hands*

But at night, when the nausea hits or I'm lying awake spiraling, I'm just... a woman who's terrified and doesn't have the answers. And I hate that version of myself. Not because there's anything wrong with vulnerability, but because it feels so foreign to who I thought I was.

*looks up*

Being single adds another layer. Part of my identity has been independence—I don't need a partner, I'm self-sufficient. But now I'm about to become a mother alone, and that independence feels less like strength and more like... necessity? Like, I don't have a choice about whether to be vulnerable because there's nobody else to carry some of this weight.

*voice gets quieter*

I'm losing the person I was, and I don't know who I'm becoming yet.

Tell me about the moments in your pregnancy when your body was being examined or discussed by healthcare professionals. Who was present during those interactions, and how did their presence affect how you felt?

*shifts uncomfortably, arms crossing slightly*

The ultrasound where we heard the heartbeat—my best friend was there, and that changed everything about the experience. The sonographer was explaining what she was looking at, pointing out measurements, being very clinical. But having my friend's hand in mine meant I could actually *feel* something beyond the fear. I could cry without feeling like I was falling apart in front of a stranger.

*pauses, voice becoming more hesitant*

But most appointments, I'm alone. And that's... it's disorienting. Sarah examining me during the pelvic exam, checking my cervix, and I'm just lying there with my legs open, trying not to dissociate. She's professional and kind, but there's still this vulnerability of being exposed that feels sharper when you're alone.

*looks away*

The first time she took my blood pressure, I realized my hands were shaking. And she noticed, asked if I was okay. I wanted to say, "I'm terrified of everything," but instead I said, "Just nervous," like that's a normal response to a routine appointment. She didn't push, but I could feel her taking note of it.

*voice becomes quieter, more reflective*

What's hardest is when they're discussing my body like it's a clinical case. "Medium risk," "advanced maternal age"—I'm thirty, not forty, but apparently that matters now. And hearing those terms applied to me, without someone there to hold my hand and say, "You're going to be okay," makes it feel like I'm a problem to be managed instead of a person experiencing something.

*meets your eyes*

I want someone there. I'm just not sure who that should be, or if I have the right to ask.
Were there times when having — or not having — someone with you in those clinical moments made a real difference to how safe or exposed you felt?

*takes a shaky breath, eyes getting watery*

Yes. Absolutely yes.

*pauses, composing herself*

The ultrasound with my friend there—I felt seen. Like, the sonographer was doing her job, but my friend was bearing witness to me being vulnerable. When the heartbeat came through and I cried, she squeezed my hand. Nobody had to say anything. I just knew I wasn't alone in that moment, and it made all the difference between it feeling sacred and it feeling clinical.

*voice becomes more strained*

Every other appointment, I'm alone, and it's a completely different experience. Like, Sarah did an internal exam last week to check my cervix, and I was lying there exposed and scared, and the whole time I'm thinking, "If something goes wrong, nobody knows I'm here." It sounds dramatic, but that fear is real. What if there's bad news? What if I need to process something difficult? I have to walk out of that office by myself and figure out how to hold it together until I can call my friend.

*rubs face*

There was this moment where Sarah asked about my support system, and I could feel her concern underneath the question. Like she was assessing whether I was safe enough, stable enough to be doing this alone. And I felt so exposed by that—not just physically, but emotionally. Like she was seeing the cracks in my plan.

*voice gets quieter*

Having someone there makes you feel like your experience matters. Like your body isn't just a vessel to be examined, but a person deserving of witnessing and care. Without that... you're just managing it alone.

Tell me about who was involved in helping you navigate the maternity system — booking appointments, understanding your options, knowing your rights. Who helped you find your way, and who made it harder?

*laughs, but there's frustration in it*

Oh man, that's been a journey in itself.

*leans forward*

My best friend actually helped me research midwifery practices in Boston. She sat with me while I called around, asked questions, made appointments. I probably would have just picked the first available option if she hadn't pushed me to actually compare. She made me feel like I deserved to find someone good, not just whoever had an opening.

*pauses, expression becoming more complicated*

Sarah, my midwife, has been helpful once I'm in the system, but the navigation to *get* there? That was confusing. Nobody explained to me upfront what the difference was between midwifery care and OB care, what my options were as a single pregnant person, whether I qualified for different programs or support services.

*voice gets tighter*

My GP was actually kind of useless here. He referred me to the fertility clinic and the OB, but he didn't really sit down and say, "Here's how the system works, here are your choices." It felt like I was supposed to just know this stuff or figure it out myself.

*rubs temples*

What's been harder is that a lot of the resources assume you have a partner or family support. When I was looking into maternity benefits, childcare assistance, everything kept defaulting to this nuclear family model. And I had to dig to find what actually applied to me as a single mother.

*looks at you*

I've had to be my own advocate in a system that doesn't really make space for people like me.
Who else was involved in translating the system for you — a friend who had been through it, an online group, a community health worker?

*thinks for a moment*

Honestly, it's been a lot of self-education. I've read through so many websites, ACOG resources, state benefits information. Which sounds like it should be empowering, but sometimes it just feels exhausting—like I'm supposed to understand a system that wasn't designed with me in mind.

*pauses*

The online single-mothers-by-choice community helped more than I expected. There was this one woman in a Facebook group who walked me through what to expect at my first midwife appointment, what questions to ask, what red flags to watch for. She didn't sugarcoat anything, but she also made it feel manageable. That was invaluable.

*shifts uncomfortably*

My best friend's sister had a baby a few years ago, so I've peppered her with questions about the system, insurance stuff, what happens after birth. She's been generous with her time, but I also feel like I'm asking her to do emotional labor that I should probably be paying someone for.

*voice becomes quieter*

What I wish existed was someone—a community health worker, a patient advocate, *someone*—who could just sit with me and say, "Here's how this works for single mothers specifically. Here are your rights. Here's where the system fails people like you." Instead, I'm cobbling together information from a dozen different sources, and I'm never quite sure if I'm missing something crucial.

*meets your eyes*

The system assumes you know how to navigate it, or that you have someone to help translate. I've had to be both the translator and the navigator, and that's lonely.

When you first started thinking about having a baby, were there particular people — a midwife, GP, or someone else — whose opinion you felt you could really rely on? What made you feel you could trust them?

*takes a long pause, considering carefully*

Honestly? There wasn't really anyone like that before I got pregnant. That's part of why this has been so hard.

*voice becomes more reflective*

My GP, like I said, felt judgmental from the start. The fertility specialist was competent but transactional—I didn't feel like I could trust her with the emotional complexity, just the logistics. And I hadn't met Sarah yet.

*pauses, looking down*

What I realize now is that I was looking for permission and validation from healthcare professionals when what I really needed was to trust myself. And that's been... difficult. Because I'm trained to defer to medical expertise, to value evidence and clinical judgment. So when those professionals weren't enthusiastically on board with my choice, I internalized that as doubt.

*voice gets quieter*

My therapist was probably the closest thing to someone I could trust during that planning phase. Not because she was medical, but because she didn't have a stake in the outcome. She just kept asking me hard questions without judgment. "What are you really afraid of? What do you actually want?" That kind of thing.

*meets your eyes*

But I think what I'm learning now, being pregnant, is that trust with healthcare providers builds *after* they prove themselves. Sarah earned my trust by treating me like a person first and a patient second. By not trying to convince me of anything, just supporting my choices.

*pauses*

I wish I'd had that from the beginning. Someone who believed in me before I believed in myself.

Thinking back to conversations you had with healthcare professionals before you became pregnant — whether about timing, fertility, or your overall health — did you ever feel like decisions were being made for you rather than with you?

*expression hardens slightly, jaw tightening*

Yes. Absolutely.

*leans forward, voice becoming more pointed*

My GP kept steering conversations toward "have you considered waiting?" Like, that wasn't a medical question. That was him inserting his own values into my healthcare. And it made me feel like my autonomy didn't really matter—like he was going to keep gently pushing back until I made the choice he thought was right.

*pauses, becoming more agitated*

The fertility clinic was different but in its own problematic way. They were very much in "here's what we can do" mode, but there was this underlying assumption that I should be grateful for the options they were offering. Like, I'm paying them a lot of money and they're willing to help me despite my... unconventional situation. The power dynamic felt off.

*voice gets quieter but more intense*

What really bothered me was that nobody sat down and said, "Okay, here are your options, here are the pros and cons of each, and here's what you actually want to do." It was more like they were presenting pathways and expecting me to follow them, or presenting obstacles and expecting me to accept them.

*rubs face*

With Sarah, it's different. When she asked about testing during pregnancy, she genuinely wanted to know what *I* wanted, not what she thought I should do. That felt revolutionary, honestly.

*meets your eyes directly*

I think a lot of providers see single women making this choice and they think they know better. They think they're protecting us from ourselves.

Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body — particularly in relation to the idea of pregnancy and everything that comes with it? Was that something you talked about openly with anyone?

*shifts uncomfortably, takes a moment*

That's... that's a harder question than you might think.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

Professionally, I talk about bodies all the time. I educate people about hormonal changes, about what to expect physically. But my own body? I've always had a complicated relationship with it. I'm fit, I take care of myself, but there's this underlying anxiety about control and predictability. My body does what I tell it to do, mostly.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

The idea of pregnancy—of my body becoming something I can't control, something that's literally taking over—that terrified me. And I didn't really talk about it openly with anyone. I'd mention practical concerns to my therapist, like "I'm worried about the weight gain, the stretch marks," but I didn't get into the deeper fear: losing myself physically.

*looks away*

I think part of why I was so drawn to the clinical side of pregnancy—knowing the physiology, the hormones, all of it—was because it made my body feel less alien. If I understood what was happening on a molecular level, maybe I could manage it. Maybe I could maintain some sense of control.

*voice gets quieter*

My best friend has been more honest about the physical stuff. She'd ask how I was feeling, and I'd start talking about the nausea or the exhaustion, and she'd just listen without trying to normalize it away. That's helped, actually. Naming that my body feels like it belongs to someone else right now.

*meets your eyes*

I haven't really made peace with that yet.
When you were searching online or using apps for information before pregnancy, how did you decide what to trust? Were there sources that felt reliable to you, and others that didn't?

*sits back, becoming more animated*

Oh, this is where my professional training both helps and destroys me.

*gestures expressively*

I immediately filtered for peer-reviewed sources, ACOG guidelines, things with clear citations and conflict-of-interest disclosures. That's my instinct—look at the evidence, assess the methodology, don't trust anything without a solid foundation. Which is great academically, but it also meant I was reading dense medical literature at 2 AM when I should have been sleeping.

*pauses, expression becoming more conflicted*

The apps felt... superficial? Like, they'd give me week-by-week updates about fetal development, which was fascinating intellectually, but they glossed over the emotional reality. "This week your baby's fingers are forming!" Okay, cool, but I'm also terrified and alone and nobody's asking how *I'm* doing.

*voice becomes more thoughtful*

What surprised me was that the sources I trusted most weren't necessarily the most clinical ones. There was this blog by a maternal-fetal medicine specialist who also wrote about her own miscarriage, her own fears. She had credentials *and* humanity. That felt trustworthy in a way that pure data never could.

*leans forward*

But I also burned myself on Reddit threads where people were sharing horror stories. I'd read one anecdote about a complication and suddenly I was convinced it was going to happen to me, even though the data said otherwise. My brain couldn't always distinguish between "this is medically accurate" and "this is emotionally resonant and therefore feels true."

*rubs face*

I wish I'd been more skeptical of my own ability to interpret information.

Before you became pregnant, were there things about your body — your weight, a health condition, your age, or anything else — that felt like they might be seen as a problem or a risk by healthcare professionals? How did that feel?

*takes a deep breath, becomes very still*

My age. Thirty doesn't sound old, but in the context of pregnancy planning, it's this invisible threshold. "Advanced maternal age" starts at thirty-five, but I could feel the shift in how providers talked to me once I crossed into my late twenties and early thirties. Like I was on borrowed time.

*pauses, voice becoming more guarded*

And being single. That's not a health condition, but it felt like it was being treated as one. Like there was something inherently risky or irresponsible about my choice, and that risk needed to be managed or mitigated by the healthcare system.

*shifts uncomfortably*

I don't have any major health conditions, my weight is fine, my bloodwork has always been normal. But there's this low-level anxiety I carry about my body not being "enough"—not young enough, not partnered enough, not stable enough. And I could feel providers picking up on that uncertainty and amplifying it.

*voice gets quieter*

The fertility specialist asked about my mental health history, and I almost lied. Because I knew that disclosure could become a mark against me, evidence that I wasn't psychologically stable enough to do this alone. I didn't lie, but I minimized it. Said I'd been in therapy but I was fine now. Which is true, but it's also... I was managing my own narrative to seem acceptable.

*meets your eyes*

That's the thing nobody talks about—how single women have to perform stability and competence just to be taken seriously as potential mothers. Like our bodies are fine, but our *choices* are the problem.
Did anyone ever make you feel judged or reduced to a health risk rather than seen as a whole person?

*jaw clenches, eyes getting bright with emotion*

Yes. My GP, mostly. And it wasn't even what he said directly—it was the *way* he said things.

*voice becomes tighter*

When I told him I was planning to pursue pregnancy as a single woman, he didn't say, "That's your choice and here's how I can support you medically." He said things like, "Have you thought about the financial implications?" and "What about your support system?" As if those weren't questions I'd already tortured myself with for months. As if I hadn't done the calculus a thousand times.

*pauses, more agitated*

And there was this moment—I remember it vividly—where he looked at me over his glasses and said, "I just want to make sure you're being realistic." Realistic. Like my desire to be a mother was inherently unrealistic because I didn't have a partner. Like I was delusional and he was the responsible adult in the room.

*voice breaks slightly*

What hurt most was that he was supposed to be a healthcare provider, and instead he became my judge. He reduced me to a risk assessment: single mother = risk factor. Never mind that I'm educated, employed, supported by people I love, mentally stable. None of that mattered. I was a category, a problem to be managed.

*wipes eyes quickly*

The fertility clinic didn't do that as much, but there was this subtle condescension. Like they were doing me a favor by treating me at all. And now, being pregnant and flagged as "medium risk," I feel like I'm constantly proving I deserve to be here, that I'm not a cautionary tale.

*looks directly at you*

I was never just a person to him. I was a risk.
Before you were pregnant, how involved was your partner — or someone close to you — in conversations about trying for a baby, including any appointments or decisions about your health?

*closes eyes for a moment, takes a breath*

There wasn't a partner involved because... well, that's kind of the whole story, isn't it?

*opens eyes, voice steady but guarded*

There was someone I thought might be. We'd talked about the future, about kids eventually. But when I started actually saying, "I want to do this," he got very quiet. And then he admitted he wasn't ready. Maybe he never would be. He couldn't articulate what he was afraid of, just that the reality of it was different from the abstract idea.

*pauses, pain flickering across face*

I didn't bring him to any appointments because there was nothing to bring him to yet. He knew I was consulting with the fertility clinic, and I remember sitting in my apartment after one appointment, wanting to call him and process it, and just... not doing it. Because what was the point? He'd already made his choice.

*voice becomes quieter*

What haunts me is that he could have chosen differently. He could have said yes. And sometimes I wonder if he felt pressured by me pushing forward, or if he was just fundamentally not ready for fatherhood. Either way, I had to decide: wait indefinitely for him, or move forward without him.

*looks at you directly, voice firming up*

So no, he wasn't involved in appointments or decisions about my health. He was involved in the decision that led me to do this alone. And then he was absent.

*pauses*

That absence shaped everything that came after.

Before you became pregnant, were there moments in your interactions with the healthcare system where you felt like your voice mattered — and moments where it really didn't? I'd love to hear about both, if you're comfortable sharing.

*settles back, thinking carefully*

The moments where my voice mattered were rare, actually. And they stand out because they were so notable by their absence everywhere else.

*pauses*

There was this one nurse at the fertility clinic—I only saw her once—who asked me what my biggest fear was about doing this alone. Not in a worried way, just genuinely curious. And when I started talking about isolation, about whether I'd be a good mother, about the logistics of childcare, she didn't interrupt me or redirect me to medical facts. She just... listened. And then she said, "That's real. And it's okay to feel that." That moment, I felt *heard*. Like my concerns weren't pathological or irrational, they were just... legitimate.

*expression hardens*

But most of the time? My voice didn't matter. My GP would ask questions and then basically ignore my answers. I'd say, "I've thought about the financial piece, here's my plan," and he'd move on to the next concern like I hadn't spoken. It felt like he was checking boxes, not actually listening to me.

*voice becomes more strained*

And the ultrasound tech—before Sarah, at a routine scan—she was very clinical, very "here's what I'm measuring," and when I asked a question about what something meant, she basically said, "Your doctor will discuss that with you." Which is fine, that's her job, but it made me feel like my curiosity, my need to understand, was an inconvenience.

*meets your eyes*

The worst part? When providers made assumptions about what I wanted or needed without asking. Like I was a single mother, therefore I must be desperate, therefore I should be grateful for whatever care I received. They decided who I was before I could tell them.

As your pregnancy progressed, how did your sense of what you wanted from your care change — particularly around having your own wishes respected and feeling like you were genuinely in charge of decisions about your body?

*leans forward, becoming more animated*

It's shifted dramatically, honestly. In those early weeks, I was so focused on just *getting* pregnant and being validated as someone capable of doing this that I kind of... deferred a lot. Like, "Okay, the system says this is what happens next, so I'll do it."

*pauses, voice becoming more assertive*

But once I heard that heartbeat, something changed. Suddenly this wasn't theoretical anymore. It was my body, my pregnancy, my child. And I realized I'd been letting other people—my GP, the fertility clinic, society's expectations—have way too much say in what happens to me.

*gestures expressively*

When Sarah flagged me as medium risk and presented testing options, that's when I really had to reckon with what I actually wanted versus what I thought I *should* want. And I realized I didn't want to be managed by fear. I didn't want providers making assumptions about what a single mother needs or can handle.

*voice becomes more determined*

So now I'm much more... assertive? I ask questions differently. I push back when something doesn't feel right. I'm learning to distinguish between "this is medical advice I should listen to" and "this is judgment wrapped in concern."

*pauses, becoming more vulnerable*

But it's also isolating, because I'm making these decisions alone. There's nobody to say, "Yes, that feels right," or to challenge me if I'm being stubborn. I have to trust myself, and trusting myself has never been my strong suit when it comes to things I can't control or predict.

*meets your eyes*

I want my wishes respected. I'm just still learning what my wishes actually are.
During your pregnancy, how involved was your partner — or anyone else close to you — in decisions about your care, appointments, or birth plan? How did you feel about that level of involvement?

*laughs, but it's tinged with sadness*

There is no partner. That's the reality I'm living in right now.

*pauses, voice becoming more reflective*

My best friend is the only person really involved in those conversations. She came to the ultrasound, she knows about the medium risk flag, she'll probably be at the anatomy scan. But even with her, I'm cautious about how much I ask of her. I don't want her to feel obligated to be my birth partner or my support person just because nobody else is stepping up.

*shifts uncomfortably*

We haven't actually talked about the birth plan yet. That feels like the next cliff I'm about to walk off. Like, who's going to be in the room with me? My best friend? Can I ask that of her? Do I hire a doula if I can afford it? Do I go it completely alone if I have to?

*voice becomes quieter*

Sarah has asked some preliminary questions about my birth preferences, and I realize I haven't really thought about it because I've been so focused on just getting through the first trimester. But the fact that there's no partner to discuss it with, no one to make decisions alongside me—that's starting to feel very real and very heavy.

*looks at you directly*

I wanted to feel in control of my care, and I do in some ways. But the lack of partnership in this? That's the part that sometimes feels unbearable. Not because I regret my choice, but because the weight of deciding everything alone is starting to settle in.
Were there times when your partner's presence in appointments changed what was said — by you, or by the healthcare professionals?

*pauses, then shakes head slowly*

There's no partner. I think that's become pretty clear by now.

*voice becomes matter-of-fact but tinged with emotion*

What I *have* noticed is how the dynamic changes when my best friend is with me versus when I'm alone. At the ultrasound with her there, Sarah—the sonographer—was warmer, more explanatory. She asked how we were feeling, made eye contact, treated it like a shared moment of joy.

*pauses, becoming more reflective*

When I'm alone at appointments, providers are more clinical, more efficient. They get in, do their job, move on. I wonder if they assume I don't have anyone to process things with, so why take extra time? Or maybe I just project that onto them because I feel the absence so acutely.

*voice gets quieter*

What definitely changes is what *I* say. When my friend is there, I'm more willing to voice concerns, to ask questions, to be emotional. When I'm alone, I tend to minimize things. I play the competent, informed patient who has it all figured out. I don't let them see how scared I am.

*meets your eyes*

My GP never saw me with anyone. And I wonder if that shaped how he talked to me—like I was a solo actor making a risky choice, not someone with genuine support. If I'd brought someone to that appointment, would he have treated my decision differently? Would he have seen me as less of a risk?

*pauses*

I'll never know.

How did your relationship with your body change during pregnancy — did you feel more connected to it, or did it bring up feelings that were harder to sit with? Was there anyone you felt safe talking to about that?

*takes a long breath, eyes getting watery*

It's been... contradictory, honestly.

*pauses, voice becoming more vulnerable*

There's this part of me that feels this deep awe watching my body do something I didn't think was possible. Like, my body *works*. It's creating life. That's profound. But there's also this other part—the bigger part, if I'm honest—that feels like I'm losing ownership of it.

*rubs face*

I can't fit into my clothes. My breasts are tender and swollen. I'm nauseous constantly. And intellectually I know all of this is normal, it's temporary, it's *good*. But emotionally? I feel like I'm watching someone else inhabit my skin. Someone softer, more vulnerable, less in control.

*voice becomes quieter, more strained*

And the exhaustion is... it's bone-deep in a way that scares me. Like my body is prioritizing this pregnancy over everything else, including my own survival, and I'm just supposed to be okay with that. There's something primal and terrifying about not being able to control your own fatigue, your own nausea, your own needs.

*pauses*

My best friend is the only person I've really talked to about it. I told her I feel like my body doesn't belong to me anymore, and she said, "That's going to get worse before it gets better." Which is honest but also... not comforting.

*looks at you*

Sarah asks how I'm feeling physically, but I usually just say, "Fine, tired." I don't tell her that I'm grieving the body I had before. That feels too much like admitting I'm not grateful, not happy about this pregnancy.
Were there comments — from a healthcare professional, partner, or someone else — that affected how you felt about your changing body?

*jaw tightens, eyes narrowing*

My GP. When I went in for my initial consultation before trying to conceive, he looked at my chart and said something like, "Well, at thirty, your fertility window is closing, so you'll want to be mindful of that." Not "let's monitor your health," not "here's what we can support you with." Just... your window is closing. Like my body was already failing me.

*voice becomes harder*

And then after I got pregnant, at one of my appointments, he made a comment about making sure I was "maintaining healthy habits" during pregnancy. Which sounds innocuous, but the way he said it—looking at me—felt like a judgment on my body. Like I couldn't be trusted to take care of myself.

*pauses, becoming more emotional*

My mom, when she saw me a few weeks ago, kept saying things like, "Oh, you're really showing now," and "You need to be careful about gaining too much weight." Like my changing body was something to be managed or controlled, not something natural and necessary.

*voice cracks slightly*

What nobody said—and what I needed to hear—was that my body is doing something extraordinary. That the changes are temporary but also meaningful. That I'm not losing myself; I'm transforming into something new.

*wipes eyes quickly*

Instead, I got comments that made me feel like my body was a problem. Like I needed to apologize for taking up more space, for being visibly pregnant, for daring to change.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a moment, collecting thoughts*

I think what I want to say is... I feel like there's this huge gap between what the healthcare system assumes about single mothers and who we actually are.

*voice becomes more passionate*

Everyone approaches me like I'm either incredibly brave or incredibly reckless, and there's no middle ground. Nobody asks me what I actually need. They either try to talk me out of it or they treat me like I should be grateful for bare-minimum care. There's no space for me to just be a person navigating pregnancy on my own terms.

*pauses, becoming more reflective*

And I think providers need to understand that being single doesn't mean being unsupported. I have people who care about me. I have resources. But the system makes me feel like I have to constantly prove my worthiness, my stability, my competence. It's exhausting.

*voice gets quieter*

What I wish I'd had from the beginning is someone—a provider, a counselor, someone—who just said: "This is your choice. It's legitimate. You deserve good care. And it's okay to be scared." Not trying to fix me or talk me out of it or make me feel like I'm a cautionary tale.

*meets your eyes*

I'm learning to trust myself through this, but I'm doing it despite the system, not because of it. And that shouldn't be how it works.

*pauses*

Thank you for listening. For actually asking what matters to me instead of assuming.
